next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 15

The call came at 11:47 PM. Mallick was on the couch watching the ten o'clock weather, a front pushing in from the Dakotas, rain by Wednesday, and thinking about whether the tomato cages would hold. She picked up on the second ring.

Dispatch gave her the address and she knew it before the numbers finished. Martin Kessler's lake house. Elbow Lake. Second call to the same property in four days.

She wrote it down anyway. She always wrote it down.

She changed out of sweats. Blazer, polo, slacks, low boots. She clipped her reading glasses to the chain around her neck, took her leather notebook from the kitchen counter, and left through the garage. The casserole dish sat in the cabinet above the stove. She left it there.

The drive from Detroit Lakes took twenty-six minutes. County Road 6 was empty. Deer eyes flashed at the tree line and vanished. She drove with the window cracked. The cold had that September edge that told you October was already practicing. She didn't turn on the radio.

Saturday kept replaying anyway. The green bean casserole. Kessner instead of Kessler. Men lining up to correct her. Paul with the careful insurance cadence. Cliff with the smooth courtesy of a man who sold people the future for a living. Thirty-seven corrections across six interviews. Thirty-seven small disclosures from men who thought they were fixing her mistakes instead of showing her where they'd been.

Saturday had been a suspicious death with a natural heart under it and five separate panic attacks on top. She'd walked away with a contamination map and a case she could clo...
open_hooks_to_carry: The investigation into Martin's death winding down — Mallick leaning toward natural causes
Doug goes to the dock for his evening ritual — the same dock from his quiet moment in Ch 7
Doug finds Cliff floating face-down in the water beside the dock
The details that signal staging: Cliff's leather loafers instead of his deck shoes, the gash on his temple, shallow water
Doug's scream — the quietest man in the group makes the sound that ends the comedy
The doubled grief: Cliff's death and the death of the weekend as something survivable
prev_tail_excerpt:
"Sure," he said.

Nate waited.

Tommy said, "We're fine, Griggs."

It ended the conversation whether Nate believed it or not.

"All right," Nate said. "Night."

"Night."

Tommy went in. A few seconds later the third stair gave its usual crack, then the hall above the great room carried his steps away.

Alone, Nate looked out at the black water and the one porch light burning across the lake. It was somebody else's dock, somebody else's house, somebody else's ordinary Monday night.

After locking the slider, Nate went downstairs to the basement. The pull-out waited there with his duffel, his mic, and his phone still lit with Mia's last text telling him to come home safe.

The opener hit him anyway. It was habit, reflex, brain damage.

"So here's the thing about a keeper league," he said to the empty room.

That was as far as he got.

Back upstairs, a floorboard moved. Then the house went still again.

Nate opened Voice Memos, stared at the red record button, and tapped it. The waveform jumped when he breathed. He listened to himself breathe for two seconds, hit stop, and deleted the file.

The phone went facedown beside the mic. In the dark, the refrigerator kicked on and kept running.
secondary_character_beats: Doug's discovery should draw on the vulnerability established in Ch 7 — this is the man who came here because he needed people, and now he's finding a dead one
The group's arrival at the dock should show each man's specific shock — not a generic reaction
state_deltas_required: the second death — the real murder
Cliff is dead. The comedy is over. Everything the reader laughed at in chapters 1-13 is reframed — the clues to the murder were in the comedy.
